papergraph.title: SPECIFIC FEATURES OF HYDROMECHANICAL SYNTHESIS OF VANADIUM PENTAOXIDE NANOBELTS
papergraph.citation: D. Kolomiiets et al. (2022) SPECIFIC FEATURES OF HYDROMECHANICAL SYNTHESIS OF VANADIUM PENTAOXIDE NANOBELTS doi:10.31651/2076-5851-2022-3-14
papergraph.venue: Cherkasy University Bulletin Physical and Mathematical Sciences
papergraph.abstract:
This study offers a comprehensive analysis of the hydromechanical synthesis process for one-dimensional vanadium pentoxide (V₂O₅) nanobelts. The research aims to develop a scalable, ambient-condition methodology to produce nanostructured materials for advanced energy storage applications. Aqueous suspensions containing commercial V₂O₅ powder and varying concentrations of sodium chloride (NaCl) were subjected to intensive magnetic stirring without thermal post-processing. The experimental procedure involved monitoring the evolution of physicochemical properties over time, enabling detailed insight into the crystallization and growth behavior under turbulent liquid-phase conditions.
The evolution of the suspension's chemical and physical properties was monitored using measurements of pH, viscosity, and electrical conductivity. These indicators provided valuable insight into the reaction kinetics and nucleation dynamics. pH decreased progressively during the synthesis, signifying the acidification of the medium and possible hydrolysis reactions involving vanadium species. Simultaneously, viscosity increased, indicating the formation of extended molecular networks and colloidal intermediates. The observed changes in electrical conductivity confirmed the dissolution of V₂O₅ and the subsequent saturation of the solution. These changes serve as indirect indicators of the progress of nanostructure formation.
Morphological transformation during the synthesis was documented through scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The initial commercial V₂O₅ powder consisted of coarse particles with irregular shapes and low aspect ratios. After 72 hours of hydromechanical treatment, the formation of ribbon-like nanostructures was observed. These nanobelts displayed dimensions ranging from 10–15 nm in thickness and several microns in length, with smooth surfaces and apparent alignment. The observed anisotropic growth was likely governed by crystallization along preferred lattice directions, influenced by flow patterns and ion-mediated surface processes.
X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis complemented the SEM observations. Initially, sharp diffraction peaks corresponding to the orthorhombic structure of V₂O₅ were present. With increasing treatment time, a decline in peak intensity and broadening of diffraction features were recorded. By the end of a 96-hour synthesis cycle, the crystalline signals had nearly vanished, and the patterns suggested a transition to a predominantly amorphous or nanocrystalline phase. These results underscore the mechanical disruption of long-range order induced by constant shear and collision forces in the suspension.
Electrochemical performance of the synthesized nanostructures was assessed using cyclic voltammetry (CV). Several electrode configurations were tested, including pristine graphite, graphite with untreated commercial powder, and composites with V₂O₅ nanobelts at different mass ratios. The composite electrode with a 1:1 mass ratio of graphite and nanobelts demonstrated the most favorable electrochemical behavior. Well-defined redox peaks, high reversibility, and increased charge-storage capacity were observed in comparison with reference electrodes.
Further analysis of the CV profiles revealed rapid ion insertion/extraction kinetics, enhanced by the high surface area and one-dimensional architecture of the nanobelts. These structures facilitated efficient ion transport and minimized resistance. Additionally, the uniform dispersion of nanostructures within the graphite matrix improved electronic connectivity and mechanical integrity. The findings highlight the synergy between nanostructured V₂O₅ and conductive matrices in optimizing electrochemical performance.
Beyond energy storage, the synthesized V₂O₅ nanobelts exhibit promising characteristics for applications in chemical sensing, electrocatalysis, and photonic devices. Their high surface-to-volume ratio, redox activity, and structural flexibility make them suitable candidates for multifunctional systems. The ability to tune the synthesis parameters – such as salt concentration, stirring speed, and process duration-enables control over product morphology, crystallinity, and functional behavior.
The use of NaCl as a catalytic additive is of particular interest. It accelerates the breakdown of V₂O₅ agglomerates and promotes nucleation. The presence of chloride ions may alter the ionic strength and dielectric environment of the suspension, facilitating enhanced diffusion and templated growth. These effects warrant further investigation, particularly regarding ion-specific interactions and their implications for crystal engineering.
From a methodological perspective, the study showcases how mechanical forces—traditionally overlooked in crystallization – can direct nanoscale material formation under mild conditions. The interplay between turbulence, shear stress, and particle-solution interactions contributes to a new paradigm in low-temperature nanomaterials synthesis. This paradigm avoids complex autoclave systems or hazardous reagents, offering a sustainable alternative for industry and research.
The experimental framework developed here may be adapted to other metal oxide systems. For instance, analogous hydromechanical protocols could be applied to synthesize nanostructured TiO₂, ZnO, or Fe₂O₃. Moreover, the incorporation of dopants or functionalizing agents into the suspension could further expand the utility of the process. The hydromechanical route offers excellent compatibility with green chemistry principles, potentially reducing waste and energy consumption.
The findings also open avenues for integrating such nanomaterials into hybrid energy storage systems, such as supercapacitor–battery hybrids or solid-state cells. The scalability of the method, combined with its reproducibility and material efficiency, enhances its appeal for applied research and pre-industrial prototyping. Furthermore, the technique may be tailored for batch or continuous-flow processing, enabling production at various scales.
In summary, this study delivers an extensive characterization of a low-temperature, solution-based strategy for synthesizing V₂O₅ nanobelts. It demonstrates the critical role of hydrodynamic conditions and salt-mediated mechanisms in controlling the structure and performance of nanomaterials. The synthesized nanobelts exhibit favorable physical and electrochemical properties, confirming their suitability for energy-related technologies. The approach represents a significant advancement in scalable nanomaterials fabrication and paves the way for further studies in hydrodynamically controlled synthesis strategies.
strengths: scalable ambient-condition synthesis
avoids autoclave systems or hazardous reagents
high surface area and one-dimensional architecture enabling rapid ion kinetics
synergy with conductive matrices improving electronic connectivity and mechanical integrity
compatibility with green chemistry principles
improvements: tuning salt concentration, stirring speed, and process duration for morphology control

papergraph.title: Interfacial Engineering of MXene‐Based Heterogeneous Membranes with Enhanced Ion Selectivity in Vanadium Redox Flow Battery
papergraph.citation: Yanbo Zhang et al. (2025) Interfacial Engineering of MXene‐Based Heterogeneous Membranes with Enhanced Ion Selectivity in Vanadium Redox Flow Battery doi:10.1002/adfm.202510597
papergraph.abstract: Interfacial interactions profoundly influence the proton conductivity and ion selectivity of ion‐exchange membranes. However, previous studies on membranes primarily focus on enhancing the selective‐conductive properties of the secondary phase while neglecting interfacial regulation. In this study, PFSA/CF3SO3‐M heterogeneous membrane is successfully fabricated by incorporating CF3SO3‐M nanosheets—derived from organic Lewis acid (trifluoromethanesulfonic acid) etching of Ti3AlC2—into perfluorosulfonic acid resin (PFSA). Compared to PFSA/HF‐M hybrid membrane containing fillers etched by inorganic acid (HF), the PFSA/CF3SO3‐M heterogeneous membrane exhibits superior proton conductivity, reduces vanadium ion permeability, and the highest ion selectivity (11.7 S min cm−3) among all tested membranes. This enhancement arises from two mechanisms: the development of heterointerface proton transport channels between filler and PFSA matrix and the pH‐responsive Donnan exclusion effect inherent to the filler. Electrochemical evaluations demonstrate that the PFSA/CF3SO3‐M membrane outperforms conventional Nafion membrane in vanadium redox flow battery (VRFB) applications, achieving 98.1% coulombic efficiency and 84.2% energy efficiency after 800 cycles. Cost analysis reveals that the PFSA/CF3SO3‐M heterogeneous membrane demonstrates a 59.8% reduction in membrane cost compared to Nafion 212 in a 2 MW × 6 h VRFB system. This work establishes an effective strategy for designing MXene‐based ion‐selective membranes through rational interfacial engineering.
improvements: No explicit limitation extracted.
papergraph.contribution: Rational interfacial engineering enables enhanced ion selectivity and proton conductivity in MXene-based heterogeneous membranes for VRFBs, via heterointerface proton transport channels and pH-responsive Donnan exclusion.
papergraph.limitation: However, previous studies on membranes primarily focus on enhancing the selective‐conductive properties of the secondary phase while neglecting interfacial regulation.

papergraph.title: Balanced-state electrolytes overcome crossover in vanadium redox flow batteries
papergraph.citation: Zhenyu Wang et al. (2026) Balanced-state electrolytes overcome crossover in vanadium redox flow batteries doi:10.1038/s41467-026-70872-8
papergraph.abstract: Flow batteries are a leading large-scale energy storage technology, valued for inherent safety and scalability. However, active species cross over the membrane—beyond intended charge carriers— resulting in rapid capacity decay and hindering further development. Traditional approaches to mitigate capacity decay focus on increasing membrane ion selectivity, but this typically compromises power density. Here, we introduce a balanced-state electrolyte strategy that departs from traditional symmetric electrolyte designs by independently tuning both concentration and valence. This approach enables precise control over transmembrane ion flux, thereby maintaining the dynamic equilibrium of active species and effectively reducing capacity decay. Vanadium flow battery tests demonstrate that this approach overcomes the traditional trade-off between proton conductivity and ion selectivity: a battery employing a 15 μm-thick Nafion membrane with balanced-state electrolytes achieves a 75.4% reduction in capacity decay rate— from 0.061% to 0.015% per cycle over 1,000 cycles —compared to a system using a 183 μm-thick Nafion membrane with traditional electrolytes. This method shows the potential to lower the capital cost of a 1 MW/4 MWh flow battery system by over 41.7%. Crucially, the balanced-state electrolyte approach circumvents existing membrane-related constraints in redox flow battery development and establishes a framework for advanced electrolyte design. In vanadium flow batteries, crossovers are critical challenges for membrane design. Here, authors propose a balanced state electrolyte strategy that overcomes crossovers, thereby circumventing existing membrane-related constraints and establishing a new framework for advanced electrolyte design.
strengths: overcomes trade-off between proton conductivity and ion selectivity
reduces capacity decay rate by 75.4%
lowers capital cost by over 41.7%
circumvents membrane-related constraints
establishes framework for advanced electrolyte design

papergraph.title: Effect of electrode porosity on the charge transfer in vanadium redox flow battery
papergraph.citation: Aumber Abbas et al. (2021) Effect of electrode porosity on the charge transfer in vanadium redox flow battery doi:10.1016/J.JPOWSOUR.2020.229411
papergraph.abstract: Abstract Vanadium redox flow battery (VRB) has attracted enormous attention as a large-scale energy storage system owing to its long life, stable capacity and high safety. The electrode porosity plays a crucial role in the performance of VRB. This work reports the effect of electrode porosity on the electrocatalytic activity and charge transfer in VRB using spent tea derived porous carbon as electrode material. The electrode porous microstructure is systematically modified and specific surface area is enhanced from ~10 to ~2085 m2 g⁻1, with microporosity up to ~1930 m2 g⁻1. The electrochemical performance of modified electrodes is comprehensively investigated by cyclic voltammetry, impedance spectroscopy and galvanostatic charge/discharge studies. The modified electrode with high degree of porosity improves the charge transfer process and exhibits lower charge transfer resistance with higher electrocatalytic activity for vanadium redox reactions. Moreover, the VRB static cell performance demonstrates superior energy, voltage and coulombic efficiencies for modified electrodes than the pristine graphite electrode at all tested current densities. The results suggest that the tailored porosity substantially influences the charge migration and significantly improves the VRB performance.

papergraph.title: Redox-targeted catalysis for vanadium redox-flow batteries
papergraph.citation: Feifei Zhang et al. (2018) Redox-targeted catalysis for vanadium redox-flow batteries doi:10.1016/J.NANOEN.2018.07.058
papergraph.abstract: Abstract Vanadium redox-flow battery (VRB) as a promising electrochemical power source for large-scale energy storage, suffers from various polarization losses despite that it has been extensively studied in the past decades. Among these losses, the sluggish interfacial charge transfer of the vanadium species on the respective electrode renders large overpotentials giving rise to inevitable hydrogen and oxygen evolutions during the charging process. In this study, we report an unprecedented method based on the redox targeting concept to tackle the above issues. Prussian blue (PB) and a Prussian blue analogues (PBA) with identical redox potentials to VO2+/VO2+ and V2+/V3+ are grafted on cathode and anode, respectively. Upon operation, the reversible proton-coupled redox targeting reactions between PB and VO2+/VO2+ on cathode, PBA and V2+/V3+ on anode facilitate the interfacial charge transfer of the vanadium species and concomitantly inhibit the hydrogen and oxygen evolutions, which improves the selectivity of the redox reactions and considerably enhances the round-trip energy efficiency and cycling performance of VRB in a wide range of current densities. The above redox-assisted catalytic reactions were scrutinized and the mechanisms are unequivocally manifested with various electrochemical and spectroscopic measurements. We anticipate the surface immobilized redox catalysis approach demonstrated here would generically provide a paradigm for improving the sluggish kinetic processes in a variety of electrochemical devices.

papergraph.title: A comparative study of iron-vanadium and all-vanadium flow battery for large scale energy storage
papergraph.citation: Hui Chen et al. (2022) A comparative study of iron-vanadium and all-vanadium flow battery for large scale energy storage doi:10.1016/j.cej.2021.132403
papergraph.abstract: The flow battery employing soluble redox couples for instance the all-vanadium ions and iron-vanadium ions, is regarded as a promising technology for large scale energy storage, benefited from its numerous advantages of long cycle life, high energy efficiency and independently tunable power and energy. An open-ended question associated with iron-vanadium and all-vanadium flow battery is which one is more suitable and competitive for large scale energy storage applications. This work attempts to answer this question by means of a comprehensively comparative study with respects to the electrochemical properties, charging-discharging tests, cycling performances and capital cost. The results shown that: i) the overall electrochemical properties of the two batteries are similar because of the limitation of the same negative couple; ii) the iron-vanadium flow battery is of lower energy efficiencies especially at high current densities (9% less at 150 mA cm-2), but superior self-discharge property; iii) the efficiencies of the two batteries are both of outstanding stabilities during long-term running, while the capacity of iron-vanadium flow battery is less stable; iv) The capacity of iron-vanadium flow battery can be recovered by renew the positive electrolyte with acceptable expenses; v) the iron-vanadium flow battery is cost-effective for long duration applications.
strengths: outstanding stability of efficiencies during long-term running
superior self-discharge property of iron-vanadium flow battery
cost-effectiveness of iron-vanadium flow battery for long duration applications
improvements: capacity recovery of iron-vanadium flow battery via positive electrolyte renewal
papergraph.contribution: Iron-vanadium flow battery is cost-effective for long duration applications but exhibits lower energy efficiency at high current densities and less stable capacity compared to all-vanadium flow battery.

papergraph.title: Multiple‐dimensioned defect engineering for graphite felt electrode of vanadium redox flow battery
papergraph.citation: Yingqiao Jiang et al. (2024) Multiple‐dimensioned defect engineering for graphite felt electrode of vanadium redox flow battery doi:10.1002/cey2.537
papergraph.abstract: The scarcity of wettability, insufficient active sites, and low surface area of graphite felt (GF) have long been suppressing the performance of vanadium redox flow batteries (VRFBs). Herein, an ultra‐homogeneous multiple‐dimensioned defect, including nano‐scale etching and atomic‐scale N, O co‐doping, was used to modify GF by the molten salt system. NH4Cl and KClO3 were added simultaneously to the system to obtain porous N/O co‐doped electrode (GF/ON), where KClO3 was used to ultra‐homogeneously etch, and O‐functionalize electrode, and NH4Cl was used as N dopant, respectively. GF/ON presents better electrochemical catalysis for VO2+/VO2+ and V3+/V2+ reactions than only O‐functionalized electrodes (GF/O) and GF. The enhanced electrochemical properties are attributed to an increase in active sites, surface area, and wettability, as well as the synergistic effect of N and O, which is also supported by the density functional theory calculations. Further, the cell using GF/ON shows higher discharge capacity, energy efficiency, and stability for cycling performance than the pristine cell at 140 mA cm−2 for 200 cycles. Moreover, the energy efficiency of the modified cell is increased by 9.7% from 55.2% for the pristine cell at 260 mA cm−2. Such an ultra‐homogeneous etching with N and O co‐doping through “boiling” molten salt medium provides an effective and practical application potential way to prepare superior electrodes for VRFB.

papergraph.title: Experimental Validation of Side Reaction on Capacity Fade of Vanadium Redox Flow Battery
papergraph.citation: Zebo Huang et al. (2024) Experimental Validation of Side Reaction on Capacity Fade of Vanadium Redox Flow Battery doi:10.1149/1945-7111/ad1ec8
papergraph.abstract: The all-vanadium redox flow battery (VRFB) is widely regarded as the most effective solution for mitigating the intermittent nature of renewable energy sources and simultaneously achieving “carbon neutrality goals.” Nevertheless, the battery’s overall performance is adversely affected by capacity loss resulting from side reactions, hence constraining its viability for usage in large-scale energy storage systems. This study proposes a novel approach that aims to minimize side reactions and mitigate capacity fade by employing an appropriate charge cut-off voltage mechanism. The experiment centers around the examination of performance indicators, including battery charge/discharge capabilities, internal resistance, capacity retention rate, and efficiency, for analytical purposes. The findings indicate that by setting the charge cut-off reference voltage at 1.65 V, the battery’s capacity can be sustained at 61.76% after 60 cycles. When the charge cut-off voltage is raised by increments of 0.1 V and 0.15 V, the corresponding reductions in battery capacity amount to 1.74% and 5.16% respectively. The aforementioned findings emphasize the significance of considering the effect of side reactions on battery capacity and implement that mechanism can significantly enhance the battery’s overall performance.

papergraph.title: Insights into the synergistic effect of ammonium and phosphate-containing additives for a thermally stable vanadium redox flow battery electrolyte
papergraph.citation: T. D. Nguyen et al. (2018) Insights into the synergistic effect of ammonium and phosphate-containing additives for a thermally stable vanadium redox flow battery electrolyte doi:10.1016/J.JPOWSOUR.2018.09.024
papergraph.abstract: In the field of large-scale electrochemical energy storage, the vanadium redox flow battery possesses many advantageous features. However, its performance is hampered by the low stability of the positive electrolyte at high temperatures, raising the necessity to use stabilizing agents. Phosphoric acid is commonly used to thermally stabilize the positive vanadium electrolyte, in place of effective hydrohalic acids additives, e.g. HCl, which have the risk of toxic halogen gas formation. However, the effectivity of phosphoric acid is restricted by its self-precipitation with V(V) ions in the sulfuric acid supported vanadium electrolyte to form VOPO4. In this study, we explore the synergistic effect of ammonium and phosphate ions and show that ammonium ions can effectively prevent the formation of VOPO4, hence leading to a significant improvement of the thermal stabilizing effectivity of ammonium phosphates over phosphoric acid. This study provides an insight into the thermal stabilizing effectivity of ammonium- and phosphate-containing additives for the positive vanadium electrolyte, which is useful for the understanding in the design of new additive formula.

papergraph.title: An Enhanced Equivalent Circuit Model of Vanadium Redox Flow Battery Energy Storage Systems Considering Thermal Effects
papergraph.citation: Binyu Xiong et al. (2019) An Enhanced Equivalent Circuit Model of Vanadium Redox Flow Battery Energy Storage Systems Considering Thermal Effects doi:10.1109/ACCESS.2019.2952212
papergraph.abstract: Thermal issue is one of the major concerns for safe, reliable, and efficient operation of the vanadium redox flow battery (VRB) energy storage systems. During the design of the operational strategy for a grid-connected VRB system, a suitable mathematical model is needed to predict the dynamic behaviors under various operating conditions. However, conventional VRB models usually neglect the impact of temperature variations on system performance. This work develops an enhanced VRB model with the consideration of the coupling effects between the electrochemical and the thermal behaviors. The proposed model consists of two equivalent circuits. First, the electrochemical behaviors of the VRB are modeled by a second-order RC network taking account of the effects of concentration variation of the vanadium ions and the electrochemical activation. Second, a third-order Cauer network is used to model the heat transfer process in the VRB system, and the dynamic thermal behaviors of stacks, pipes and heat exchangers are characterized. Well-designed experiments and particle swarm optimization algorithm are use to identify the parametric values of the developed model. The proposed modeling method was validated experimentally using a 5kW/3kWh VRB platform, and the results show that the model is capable of accurately predicting the VRB performance under variable temperature conditions. The developed coupled electro-thermal model is then used for simulating and analyzing the performance of a VRB system operated in conjunction with a wind power plant under real-world conditions.

papergraph.title: Impact of nanofluidic electrolyte on the energy storage capacity in vanadium redox flow battery
papergraph.citation: Jungmyung Kim et al. (2018) Impact of nanofluidic electrolyte on the energy storage capacity in vanadium redox flow battery doi:10.1016/J.ENERGY.2018.06.221
papergraph.abstract: The limitation of energy storage capacity in vanadium redox flow battery impedes further commercialization of the battery. The concept proposed in this study is to overcome the limit by using nanofluidic electrolytes. Multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) are chosen to disperse in electrolytes due to their high surfaces to volume ratio. Nanofluid electrolytes with three electrolyte weight percent MWCNT (0.05, 0.1, 0.2 wt%) were tested and compared with the pristine electrolyte. Half-cell test with cyclic voltammetry has shown that electrochemical reaction performance is proportional to the content of MWCNT in nanofluidic electrolytes. The redox reaction of nanofluidic electrolytes are enhanced by the increased electrochemical activity and reversibility in addition to the lower polarization effect. Meanwhile, single-cell test reveals that the optimum weight percent of nanofluidic electrolytes is 0.1% of MWCNT because the electrolyte containing 0.2% of MWCNT induces the unwanted precipitation at the electrodes during the electrochemical reaction. After completion of 62 charge/discharge cyclings, nanofluidic electrolyte with 0.1% MWCNT retains specific discharge capacity of 31.7 Ah L−1 while pristine electrolyte does 26.0 Ah L−1. This corresponds to 22% enhancement of energy storage by using the nanofluidic electrolytes. We conclude that nanofluidic electrolytes can considerably improve the energy storage capacity with optimized content of MWCNT.

papergraph.title: Analysis of Capacity Decay and Optimization of Vanadium Redox Flow Battery Based on Operating Conditions
papergraph.citation: Yupeng Wang et al. (2025) Analysis of Capacity Decay and Optimization of Vanadium Redox Flow Battery Based on Operating Conditions doi:10.1149/1945-7111/adde1a
papergraph.abstract: Vanadium redox flow battery offers significant potential for large-scale energy storage but face capacity decay challenges. In order to enhance battery performance and extend its service life in a simple yet effective manner, this study constructs a 2D model that takes into account the factors contributing to capacity decay. Building on this foundation, the effects of different operating conditions on discharge capacity loss are systematically investigated using the control variable method. In addition, through orthogonal testing and range analysis, the key factors affecting capacity retention rate were identified in descending order of influence: discharge cut-off voltage, charge cut-off voltage, and electrolyte flow rate. The optimal operating parameters were determined as charge cut-off voltage of 1.6 V, discharge cut-off voltage of 1.2 V, and electrolyte flow rate of 150 mL/min. It is indicated that the optimized model achieves 7.1% enhancement in discharge capacity retention rate after 50 cycles, albeit with a slight reduction in initial discharge capacity. The optimization measure effectively prolongs the service life of the battery and offers a novel research approach for the optimal design and operation of vanadium redox flow battery in the future.

papergraph.title: Metal–Air Batteries: From Static to Flow System
papergraph.citation: Xiaopeng Han et al. (2018) Metal–Air Batteries: From Static to Flow System doi:10.1002/aenm.201801396
papergraph.abstract: As an emerging battery technology, metal–air flow batteries inherit the advantageous features of the unique structural design of conventional redox flow batteries and the high energy density of metal–air batteries, thus showing great potential as efficient electrochemical systems for large‐scale electrical energy storage. This review summarizes the operating principles and recent progress of metal–air flow batteries from a materials and chemistry perspective, with particular emphasis on the latest advanced materials design and cell configuration engineering, which the authors divide into three categories based on the anode species: vanadium–air, zinc–air, and lithium–air flow batteries. Since some of the capabilities developed for metal–air static batteries can be leveraged for next‐generation flow systems, classical works on conventional metal–air batteries are selected and compared with the metal–air flow systems, highlighting the prominent advantages of the latter in achieving high energy capacity and long cycle performance. At the end, a general perspective on current challenges/opportunities and future research directions to promote the commercial application of the metal–air flow battery technology is provided. The aim is to provide a comprehensive overview and to set up a road map for guiding development from conventional static to advanced flow technologies of metal–air batteries.

papergraph.title: Contrasting Miscibility of Ionic Liquid Membranes for Nearly Perfect Proton Selectivity in Aqueous Redox Flow Batteries
papergraph.citation: Jungho Lee et al. (2023) Contrasting Miscibility of Ionic Liquid Membranes for Nearly Perfect Proton Selectivity in Aqueous Redox Flow Batteries doi:10.1002/adfm.202306633
papergraph.abstract: Ion‐selective membranes are widely used in various energy storage applications. However, conventional polymer‐based ion‐selective membranes, such as Nafion, are not perfectly ion‐selective, leading to a significant permeation of undesirable ionic species. As the imperfect ion‐selectivity of membranes leads to the failure of energy storage devices, much effort is devoted to improving membrane ion‐selectivity. Herein, an immiscible liquid‐state membrane is introduced for aqueous redox flow batteries. As hydrophobic ionic liquids are immiscible with aqueous catholyte and anolyte solutions, they separate the two without crossover. This property renders them suitable for use as a membrane for aqueous redox flow batteries. In addition, ionic liquids with long side alkyl chains, such as 1‐hexyl‐3‐methylimidazolium bis(trifluoromethylsulfonyl)imide (HMIM‐TFSI), are miscible with sulfuric acid, whereas transition metal sulfates remain insoluble in HMIM‐TFSI. For this reason, HMIM‐TFSI is selectively permeable to protons and remains impermeable to transition metal cations. As a result, the HMIM‐TFSI membrane is almost perfectly proton‐selective, leading to negligible permeability of unfavorable ionic species, such as transition metal cations. Eventually, the HMIM‐TFSI membrane shows the excellent electrochemical performance of vanadium redox flow batteries, such as negligible self‐discharge over 2800 h, high Coulombic efficiency (≈99%), and stable capacity retention over 100 cycles.

papergraph.title: Design of A Two-Stage Control Strategy of Vanadium Redox Flow Battery Energy Storage Systems for Grid Application
papergraph.citation: Binyu Xiong et al. (2022) Design of A Two-Stage Control Strategy of Vanadium Redox Flow Battery Energy Storage Systems for Grid Application doi:10.1109/TSTE.2022.3181751
papergraph.abstract: The low energy conversion efficiency of the vanadium redox flow battery (VRB) system poses a challenge to its practical applications in grid systems. The low efficiency is mainly due to the considerable overpotentials and parasitic losses in the VRB cells when supplying highly dynamic charging and discharging power for grid regulation. Apart from material and structural advancements, improvements in operating strategies are equally essential for achieving the expected high-performance VRB system, although an optimized solution has not been fully exploited in the existing studies. In this paper, a two-stage control strategy is thus developed based on a proposed and experimental validated multi-physics multi-time-scale electro-thermo-hydraulic VRB model. Specifically, in the first stage, the optimal flow rate of the VRB is obtained based on online optimization to reduce parasitic loss and enhance instantaneous system efficiency, and the result serves as the set point of a feedback flow rate controller. In the second stage, dual time scales are specifically considered. And the current and flow rate controllers are designed to meet the highly varying power demands for grid-connected applications. The effectiveness of the proposed control strategy is verified under a scenario to smooth wind power generation. Comparative studies show that compared to the prevailing approaches, higher efficiency can be achieved in tracking the theoretical optimal power profiles for online battery control.

papergraph.title: Vanadium Redox Flow Battery: Review and Perspective of 3D Electrodes
papergraph.citation: Lingzhi Ye et al. (2024) Vanadium Redox Flow Battery: Review and Perspective of 3D Electrodes. doi:10.1021/acsnano.4c06675
papergraph.abstract: Vanadium redox flow battery (VRFB) has garnered significant attention due to its potential for facilitating the cost-effective utilization of renewable energy and large-scale power storage. However, the limited electrochemical activity of the electrode in vanadium redox reactions poses a challenge in achieving a high-performance VRFB. Consequently, there is a pressing need to assess advancements in electrodes to inspire innovative approaches for enhancing electrode structure and composition. This work categorizes three-dimensional (3D) electrodes derived from materials such as foam, biomass, and electrospun fibers. By employing a flexible electrode design and compositional functionalization, high-speed mass transfer channels and abundant active sites for vanadium redox reactions can be created. Furthermore, the incorporation of 3D electrocatalysts into the electrodes is discussed, including metal-based, carbon-based, and composite materials. The strong interaction and ordered arrangement of these nanocomposites have an influence on the uniformity and stability of the surface charge distribution, thereby enhancing the electrochemical performance of the composite electrodes. Finally, the challenges and perspectives of VRFB are explored through advancements in 3D electrodes, 3D electrocatalysts, and mechanisms. It is hoped that this review will inspire the development of methodology and concept of 3D electrodes in VRFB, so as to promote the future development of scientific energy storage and conversion technology.

keynote.title: SPECIFIC FEATURES OF HYDROMECHANICAL SYNTHESIS OF VANADIUM PENTAOXIDE NANOBELTS
keynote.core_problem: Scalable, low-temperature synthesis of one-dimensional V₂O₅ nanobelts for energy storage without thermal post-processing or hazardous reagents.
contributions: Development of a scalable, ambient-condition hydromechanical synthesis route for V₂O₅ nanobelts
Demonstration that mechanical forces (stirring-induced shear/turbulence) alone can drive anisotropic nanobelt formation and phase evolution
Identification of NaCl as a catalytic additive enabling agglomerate breakdown and templated nucleation
Correlation of real-time physicochemical monitoring (pH, viscosity, conductivity) with nanostructure evolution
Establishment of structure–property links: nanobelt morphology and amorphous/nanocrystalline character enable enhanced electrochemical performance
methods: Hydromechanical synthesis via intensive magnetic stirring of aqueous V₂O₅ + NaCl suspensions at ambient temperature
Time-resolved monitoring of pH, viscosity, and electrical conductivity
Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) for morphological evolution
X-ray diffraction (XRD) for crystallinity assessment
Cyclic voltammetry (CV) for electrochemical evaluation of graphite–nanobelt composites
experiments_or_evidence: SEM showing transformation from coarse irregular V₂O₅ particles to 10–15 nm thick, several-micron-long nanobelts after 72 h
XRD showing progressive peak broadening and near disappearance of crystalline signals after 96 h, indicating transition to amorphous/nanocrystalline phase
pH decrease over time indicating acidification and hydrolysis of vanadium species
Viscosity increase suggesting formation of extended molecular networks/colloidal intermediates
Electrical conductivity changes confirming dissolution and saturation of vanadium species
CV showing superior redox reversibility and charge capacity for 1:1 graphite:nanobelt electrode vs. controls
assumptions: Anisotropic growth is governed by preferential crystallization along lattice directions under hydrodynamic flow
Chloride ions modify ionic strength and dielectric environment to facilitate diffusion and templated growth
Mechanical shear and collision forces are sufficient to disrupt long-range order without thermal input
limitations: No quantification of nanobelt yield or mass balance

keynotes.title: Balanced-state electrolytes overcome crossover in vanadium redox flow batteries

keynote.title: Balanced-state electrolytes overcome crossover in vanadium redox flow batteries
keynote.core_problem: Active species crossover through the membrane in vanadium redox flow batteries causes rapid capacity decay, hindering scalability and commercial viability.
contributions: Introduction of a balanced-state electrolyte strategy that independently tunes concentration and valence of active species.
Demonstration of decoupling the trade-off between proton conductivity and ion selectivity.
Establishment of a new framework for advanced electrolyte design that circumvents membrane-related constraints.
methods: Independent tuning of electrolyte concentration and valence state to achieve dynamic equilibrium of transmembrane ion flux.
Comparative testing using Nafion membranes of differing thicknesses (15 μm vs. 183 μm) with balanced-state versus traditional symmetric electrolytes.
experiments_or_evidence: Vanadium flow battery cycling over 1,000 cycles showing 75.4% reduction in capacity decay rate (from 0.061% to 0.015% per cycle).
Capital cost reduction estimate of >41.7% for a 1 MW/4 MWh system enabled by thinner membrane and improved longevity.
assumptions: Transmembrane flux of vanadium species can be precisely controlled via electrolyte composition alone.
Dynamic equilibrium of active species is sufficient to suppress net crossover-driven capacity loss.
Nafion membrane behavior (e.g., proton conductivity, vanadium permeability) remains consistent under balanced-state conditions.
limitations: No information provided on long-term chemical stability of balanced-state electrolytes (e.g., side reactions, precipitation, pH drift).
No data on performance under variable operating conditions (e.g., temperature, current density, state-of-charge extremes).
No discussion of scalability or manufacturability of balanced-state electrolyte preparation.

gap_signals.text: No data on performance under variable operating conditions (e.g., temperature, current density, state-of-charge extremes).
No discussion of scalability or manufacturability of balanced-state electrolyte preparation.
No information provided on long-term chemical stability of balanced-state electrolytes (e.g., side reactions, precipitation, pH drift).
Traditional approaches focus on membrane engineering; this work shifts focus to electrolyte-level control—highlighting underexplored degrees of freedom in redox flow battery design.
Explicitly identifies and claims to resolve the proton conductivity vs. ion selectivity trade-off—a known bottleneck in membrane-centric optimization.

important_claims.claim: Balanced-state electrolytes reduce capacity decay rate by 75.4% over 1,000 cycles compared to traditional electrolytes with thicker membrane.
important_claims.evidence: Measured decay rates: 0.061% → 0.015% per cycle in vanadium flow battery tests.

important_claims.claim: The approach enables use of a 15 μm Nafion membrane without sacrificing longevity, whereas traditional designs require 183 μm.
important_claims.evidence: Direct comparison of decay rates between 15 μm balanced-state and 183 μm traditional systems.

important_claims.claim: Capital cost of a 1 MW/4 MWh flow battery system can be lowered by over 41.7%.
important_claims.evidence: Attributed to thinner membrane, reduced balance-of-plant complexity, and extended lifetime—though detailed cost model not provided.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a generalizable electrolyte design principle—decoupling concentration and valence tuning—for mitigating crossover in other multivalent redox flow chemistries (e.g., iron-chromium, zinc-bromine), pending validation beyond vanadium systems.

keynotes.title: Effect of electrode porosity on the charge transfer in vanadium redox flow battery

keynote.title: Effect of electrode porosity on the charge transfer in vanadium redox flow battery
keynote.core_problem: How electrode porosity affects charge transfer and electrocatalytic activity in vanadium redox flow batteries (VRBs).
contributions: Demonstrates that increasing electrode porosity (via spent tea-derived porous carbon) enhances specific surface area and microporosity, improving charge transfer.
Shows reduced charge transfer resistance and higher electrocatalytic activity for vanadium redox reactions with highly porous electrodes.
Reports superior energy, voltage, and coulombic efficiencies across all tested current densities compared to pristine graphite electrodes.
methods: Systematic modification of electrode porous microstructure using spent tea-derived porous carbon.
Cyclic voltammetry (CV) to assess electrocatalytic activity.
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) to quantify charge transfer resistance.
Galvanostatic charge/discharge studies to evaluate VRB static cell performance.
experiments_or_evidence: Specific surface area increased from ~10 to ~2085 m² g⁻¹; microporosity up to ~1930 m² g⁻¹.
Lower charge transfer resistance measured via EIS.
Higher electrocatalytic activity observed in CV for vanadium redox reactions.
Superior energy, voltage, and coulombic efficiencies in static cell tests at all tested current densities.

gap_signals.text: Uses spent tea-derived carbon — scalability, reproducibility, and long-term stability under cycling not addressed.
Relies on static cell testing; no reported results from flowing electrolyte or stack-level validation.

important_claims.claim: High porosity enhances electrocatalytic activity for vanadium redox reactions.

keynotes.title: Redox-targeted catalysis for vanadium redox-flow batteries

keynote.title: Redox-targeted catalysis for vanadium redox-flow batteries
keynote.core_problem: Sluggish interfacial charge transfer of vanadium species on electrodes in VRBs, leading to large overpotentials and parasitic hydrogen/oxygen evolution during charging, reducing round-trip efficiency and cycling stability.
contributions: Introduction of a redox-targeted catalysis strategy using surface-immobilized Prussian blue (PB) and Prussian blue analogues (PBA) as interfacial mediators.
Selective grafting of PB on cathode (matched to VO2+/VO2+ redox potential) and PBA on anode (matched to V2+/V3+ redox potential).
Demonstration of reversible proton-coupled redox targeting reactions that accelerate vanadium kinetics and suppress competing gas-evolution reactions.
Experimental validation of enhanced round-trip energy efficiency and cycling performance across a wide current density range.
methods: Surface grafting of Prussian blue (PB) onto cathode and Prussian blue analogues (PBA) onto anode.
Electrochemical measurements including cyclic voltammetry, galvanostatic charge/discharge, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy.
Spectroscopic characterization (exact modalities not specified in provided text).
Analysis of proton-coupled redox targeting reactions at electrode–electrolyte interfaces.
experiments_or_evidence: Electrochemical measurements confirming accelerated interfacial charge transfer.
Suppression of hydrogen and oxygen evolution observed during charging.
Improved round-trip energy efficiency and cycling performance across a wide range of current densities.
Mechanistic insights corroborated by combined electrochemical and spectroscopic measurements.
assumptions: Redox potentials of PB and PBA can be precisely tuned to match VO2+/VO2+ and V2+/V3+ couples, respectively.
Surface-grafted redox mediators remain stable and active under VRB operating conditions (acidic, oxidative/reductive).
Proton-coupled electron transfer kinetics between immobilized mediators and vanadium species are favorable and reversible.
limitations: Long-term stability and degradation pathways of grafted PB/PBA under extended cycling not detailed in provided text.
Scalability and reproducibility of surface grafting process not addressed.
Applicability to other electrolyte chemistries or flow battery systems not demonstrated.

gap_signals.text: Applicability to other electrolyte chemistries or flow battery systems not demonstrated.
Long-term stability and degradation pathways of grafted PB/PBA under extended cycling not detailed in provided text.
Scalability and reproducibility of surface grafting process not addressed.
Lack of clear demonstration of proton-coupled redox targeting as a catalytic pathway at solid–liquid interfaces in flow batteries.
Prior approaches lack targeted, electrode-specific redox mediation matched to individual vanadium couples.
Sluggish interfacial charge transfer of vanadium species remains unresolved despite decades of VRB research.

important_claims.claim: Redox-targeted catalysis via PB and PBA grafting improves round-trip energy efficiency and cycling performance across a wide current density range.
important_claims.evidence: Electrochemical measurements showing enhanced efficiency and stability; suppression of H2/O2 evolution; corroborating spectroscopic data.

important_claims.claim: Reversible proton-coupled redox targeting reactions facilitate interfacial charge transfer and inhibit parasitic gas evolution.
important_claims.evidence: Scrutiny via electrochemical and spectroscopic measurements; unequivocal mechanistic manifestation reported.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: High — introduces a generalizable surface-immobilized redox catalysis paradigm applicable to other electrochemical devices with sluggish kinetics, especially those involving multi-step proton-coupled electron transfers.

Comparative assessment showing enhanced thermal stability with ammonium phosphates versus phosphoric acid
assumptions: VOPO4 precipitation is the primary destabilizing mechanism limiting phosphoric acid efficacy
Ammonium ions interact with V(V) or phosphate species to suppress solid-phase nucleation
limitations: No quantitative metrics (e.g., temperature thresholds, time-to-precipitation, capacity retention) provided in excerpt
No details on electrochemical performance (e.g., coulombic efficiency, voltage efficiency, cycle life) under thermal stress
No discussion of ammonium volatility or long-term compatibility with membranes/electrodes

gap_signals.text: No details on electrochemical performance (e.g., coulombic efficiency, voltage efficiency, cycle life) under thermal stress
No discussion of ammonium volatility or long-term compatibility with membranes/electrodes
No quantitative metrics (e.g., temperature thresholds, time-to-precipitation, capacity retention) provided in excerpt
No electrochemical performance data (e.g., efficiency, capacity fade) under thermal stress is provided.
Lack of reported experimental conditions (temperatures, durations, concentrations) and quantitative stability metrics.

important_claims.claim: Ammonium ions effectively prevent VOPO4 formation in sulfuric acid-supported vanadium electrolytes.
important_claims.evidence: Stated as an observed result: 'ammonium ions can effectively prevent the formation of VOPO4'.

important_claims.claim: Ammonium phosphates provide significantly improved thermal stabilizing effectiveness compared to phosphoric acid alone.
important_claims.evidence: Stated directly: 'leading to a significant improvement of the thermal stabilizing effectivity of ammonium phosphates over phosphoric acid'.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides mechanistic insight into additive synergy for electrolyte stabilization; informs rational design of non-halogen, phosphate-based stabilizers for V(V) systems; highlights ion-pairing or complexation effects as a stabilization strategy.

keynotes.title: An Enhanced Equivalent Circuit Model of Vanadium Redox Flow Battery Energy Storage Systems Considering Thermal Effects

keynote.title: An Enhanced Equivalent Circuit Model of Vanadium Redox Flow Battery Energy Storage Systems Considering Thermal Effects
keynote.core_problem: Conventional vanadium redox flow battery (VRB) models neglect the impact of temperature variations on system performance, limiting their accuracy and reliability for safe, efficient, and grid-integrated operation.
contributions: Development of an enhanced coupled electro-thermal equivalent circuit model for VRB systems

Integration of a second-order RC network for electrochemical dynamics (including vanadium ion concentration and activation effects) with a third-order Cauer network for thermal dynamics (stacks, pipes, heat exchangers)
Experimental validation on a 5kW/3kWh VRB platform under variable temperature conditions
Application of the model to simulate VRB–wind power plant integration under real-world conditions
methods: Second-order RC equivalent circuit modeling of electrochemical behavior
Third-order Cauer equivalent circuit modeling of heat transfer dynamics
Parameter identification via well-designed experiments and particle swarm optimization (PSO)
experiments_or_evidence: Experimental validation on a 5kW/3kWh VRB platform
Model accuracy assessment under variable temperature conditions

gap_signals.text: Conventional VRB models usually neglect the impact of temperature variations on system performance.

important_claims.claim: The proposed coupled electro-thermal model is capable of accurately predicting VRB performance under variable temperature conditions.
important_claims.evidence: Validated experimentally using a 5kW/3kWh VRB platform.

important_claims.claim: The model integrates electrochemical and thermal behaviors via decoupled but coupled equivalent circuits.
important_claims.evidence: Consists of a second-order RC network (electrochemical) and a third-order Cauer network (thermal), with parameters identified experimentally and via PSO.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a physically interpretable, parameter-efficient equivalent circuit framework for VRB electro-thermal co-simulation; suitable for control design, system integration studies (e.g., with renewables), and thermal-aware operational strategy development.

keynotes.title: Impact of nanofluidic electrolyte on the energy storage capacity in vanadium redox flow battery

keynote.title: Impact of nanofluidic electrolyte on the energy storage capacity in vanadium redox flow battery
keynote.core_problem: Limitation of energy storage capacity in vanadium redox flow batteries impedes commercialization.
contributions: Proposes nanofluidic electrolytes (MWCNT-dispersed) to enhance energy storage capacity.
Identifies 0.1 wt% MWCNT as optimal concentration for performance and stability.
Demonstrates 22% increase in specific discharge capacity (31.7 vs. 26.0 Ah L−1) after 62 cycles.
methods: Dispersion of multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) in vanadium electrolyte at 0.05, 0.1, and 0.2 wt%.
Half-cell cyclic voltammetry to assess electrochemical reaction performance.
Single-cell charge/discharge cycling (62 cycles) to evaluate capacity retention and stability.
experiments_or_evidence: Cyclic voltammetry shows electrochemical performance proportional to MWCNT content.
Enhanced redox reaction kinetics: increased electrochemical activity, reversibility, and reduced polarization.
Single-cell testing identifies 0.1 wt% MWCNT as optimum; 0.2 wt% causes electrode precipitation.
After 62 cycles: nanofluidic electrolyte (0.1 wt%) retains 31.7 Ah L−1; pristine retains 26.0 Ah L−1.
limitations: Precipitation observed with 0.2 wt% MWCNT, indicating narrow optimal concentration window.
No data provided on long-term stability beyond 62 cycles or scalability of nanofluid preparation.
No discussion of MWCNT dispersion stability over time or under operational shear/flow conditions.

gap_signals.text: No data provided on long-term stability beyond 62 cycles or scalability of nanofluid preparation.
No discussion of MWCNT dispersion stability over time or under operational shear/flow conditions.
Precipitation observed with 0.2 wt% MWCNT, indicating narrow optimal concentration window.
Lack of impedance spectroscopy or kinetic analysis to quantify origin of reduced polarization.
No characterization of MWCNT dispersion homogeneity, agglomeration state, or interfacial interactions with vanadium species.

datasets_or_materials: Vanadium redox flow battery electrolyte (composition unspecified)

important_claims.claim: Nanofluidic electrolyte with 0.1 wt% MWCNT yields 22% higher specific discharge capacity than pristine electrolyte after 62 cycles.
important_claims.evidence: 31.7 Ah L−1 vs. 26.0 Ah L−1 measured post-cycling.

important_claims.claim: 0.2 wt% MWCNT induces unwanted precipitation at electrodes during electrochemical reaction.
important_claims.evidence: Reported in full text excerpt and cited as reason for suboptimal performance.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides empirical baseline for nanofluid optimization in redox flow batteries; highlights concentration-dependent trade-offs between activity enhancement and physical stability—useful for designing follow-up studies on nanomaterial dispersion control and interface engineering.

keynotes.title: Analysis of Capacity Decay and Optimization of Vanadium Redox Flow Battery Based on Operating Conditions

keynote.title: Analysis of Capacity Decay and Optimization of Vanadium Redox Flow Battery Based on Operating Conditions
keynote.core_problem: Capacity decay in vanadium redox flow batteries (VRFBs) limits service life and performance stability.
contributions: Construction of a 2D model accounting for capacity decay mechanisms
Systematic investigation of operating condition effects on discharge capacity loss using control variable method
Identification of key factors affecting capacity retention rate via orthogonal testing and range analysis
Determination of optimal operating parameters: charge cut-off voltage = 1.6 V, discharge cut-off voltage = 1.2 V, electrolyte flow rate = 150 mL/min
Demonstration of 7.1% improvement in discharge capacity retention rate after 50 cycles
methods: 2D modeling incorporating capacity decay factors
Control variable method for systematic parametric analysis
Orthogonal testing and range analysis for factor prioritization
experiments_or_evidence: Quantitative comparison of discharge capacity retention rate before and after optimization (7.1% enhancement after 50 cycles)
Observation of slight reduction in initial discharge capacity post-optimization
limitations: Optimization leads to a slight reduction in initial discharge capacity
No mention of validation against experimental data or real-world deployment

gap_signals.text: No mention of validation against experimental data or real-world deployment
Optimization leads to a slight reduction in initial discharge capacity
Optimization resulted in a slight reduction in initial discharge capacity.

important_claims.claim: Discharge cut-off voltage is the most influential factor on capacity retention rate, followed by charge cut-off voltage and electrolyte flow rate.
important_claims.evidence: Identified via orthogonal testing and range analysis in descending order of influence.

important_claims.claim: Optimized operating parameters improve discharge capacity retention rate by 7.1% after 50 cycles.
important_claims.evidence: Reported quantitative result from model evaluation.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a reproducible modeling and optimization framework (2D model + orthogonal design) for parameter sensitivity analysis and operational tuning of VRFBs; highlights trade-offs between retention rate and initial capacity.

keynotes.title: Metal–Air Batteries: From Static to Flow System

keynote.title: Metal–Air Batteries: From Static to Flow System
keynote.core_problem: Bridging the gap between high-energy-density static metal–air batteries and scalable, long-cycle-life redox flow battery architectures for large-scale energy storage.
contributions: Systematic categorization of metal–air flow batteries by anode species (vanadium–air, zinc–air, lithium–air)
Comparative analysis of classical static metal–air batteries versus emerging metal–air flow systems
Synthesis of recent advances in materials design and cell configuration engineering for metal–air flow batteries
Identification of current challenges, opportunities, and future research directions toward commercialization

gap_signals.text: Commercial application remains hindered—review explicitly calls for future work to address current challenges toward commercialization.

Classical capabilities from static metal–air batteries are leveraged but not fully transferable; flow systems require re-engineering of interfaces, electrolyte management, and air electrode stability under dynamic operation.

important_claims.claim: Metal–air flow batteries combine the structural advantages of redox flow batteries with the high energy density of metal–air batteries.
important_claims.evidence: Abstract states they 'inherit the advantageous features of the unique structural design of conventional redox flow batteries and the high energy density of metal–air batteries'.

important_claims.claim: Metal–air flow systems show prominent advantages over static counterparts in energy capacity and cycle life.
important_claims.evidence: Abstract states classical static works are compared 'highlighting the prominent advantages of the latter [flow systems] in achieving high energy capacity and long cycle performance'.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a taxonomy and cross-cutting framework (anode-based classification, materials/configuration focus) for comparing static vs. flow implementations—enabling systematic benchmarking and transfer learning across metal–air battery subfields.

keynotes.title: Contrasting Miscibility of Ionic Liquid Membranes for Nearly Perfect Proton Selectivity in Aqueous Redox Flow Batteries

keynote.title: Contrasting Miscibility of Ionic Liquid Membranes for Nearly Perfect Proton Selectivity in Aqueous Redox Flow Batteries
keynote.core_problem: Imperfect ion-selectivity of conventional polymer-based membranes (e.g., Nafion) causes undesirable ionic crossover (e.g., transition metal cations), leading to failure in aqueous redox flow batteries.
contributions: Introduction of an immiscible liquid-state ionic liquid membrane (HMIM-TFSI) for aqueous redox flow batteries
Demonstration of near-perfect proton selectivity enabled by differential miscibility: HMIM-TFSI is miscible with sulfuric acid but immiscible with transition metal sulfates
Achievement of negligible self-discharge (>2800 h), ≈99% Coulombic efficiency, and stable capacity retention over 100 cycles in vanadium redox flow batteries
methods: Utilization of hydrophobic ionic liquid (HMIM-TFSI) as a liquid membrane based on immiscibility with aqueous electrolytes
Leveraging selective solubility: proton-conducting sulfuric acid dissolves in HMIM-TFSI, while transition metal sulfates do not
experiments_or_evidence: Electrochemical performance testing in vanadium redox flow batteries: self-discharge measurement over 2800 h
Coulombic efficiency measurement (≈99%)
Capacity retention monitoring over 100 cycles
assumptions: Immiscibility between HMIM-TFSI and aqueous catholyte/anolyte enables physical separation without crossover
Differential miscibility with sulfuric acid vs. transition metal sulfates underpins selective proton transport

gap_signals.text: Conventional polymer-based ion-selective membranes (e.g., Nafion) are not perfectly ion-selective, leading to significant permeation of undesirable ionic species.
Much effort is devoted to improving membrane ion-selectivity — implying existing approaches fall short of 'nearly perfect' selectivity.

datasets_or_materials: Vanadium redox flow battery electrolytes (aqueous sulfuric acid + vanadium sulfates)

important_claims.claim: HMIM-TFSI is selectively permeable to protons and impermeable to transition metal cations.
important_claims.evidence: HMIM-TFSI is miscible with sulfuric acid but transition metal sulfates remain insoluble in it.

important_claims.claim: The HMIM-TFSI membrane enables nearly perfect proton selectivity.
important_claims.evidence: Negligible permeability of transition metal cations results in negligible self-discharge over 2800 h, ≈99% Coulombic efficiency, and stable capacity retention over 100 cycles.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a material design principle—exploiting differential miscibility of ionic liquids with specific electrolyte components—for achieving ultra-selective ion transport; applicable to other aqueous electrochemical systems requiring strict ion separation.

keynotes.title: Design of A Two-Stage Control Strategy of Vanadium Redox Flow Battery Energy Storage Systems for Grid Application

keynote.title: Design of A Two-Stage Control Strategy of Vanadium Redox Flow Battery Energy Storage Systems for Grid Application
keynote.core_problem: Low energy conversion efficiency of vanadium redox flow battery (VRB) systems in grid applications, caused by overpotentials and parasitic losses under highly dynamic charging/discharging power demands.
contributions: Proposes a two-stage control strategy for VRB energy storage systems to improve energy conversion efficiency in grid applications.
Introduces an experimentally validated multi-physics, multi-time-scale electro-thermo-hydraulic VRB model.
Develops online optimization for optimal flow rate to reduce parasitic loss and enhance instantaneous system efficiency.
Designs dual-time-scale current and flow rate controllers to meet highly varying grid power demands.
methods: Multi-physics, multi-time-scale electro-thermo-hydraulic modeling of VRB.
Online optimization for flow rate set-point generation.
Feedback flow rate control (Stage 1).
Dual time-scale current and flow rate control design (Stage 2).
experiments_or_evidence: Experimental validation of the proposed VRB model.
Verification under a wind power smoothing scenario.
Comparative studies against prevailing approaches showing improved tracking of theoretical optimal power profiles.

gap_signals.text: An optimized operating strategy has not been fully exploited in existing studies.
Low efficiency poses a challenge to practical grid applications.

important_claims.claim: The two-stage control strategy achieves higher efficiency in tracking theoretical optimal power profiles compared to prevailing approaches.
important_claims.evidence: Comparative studies show higher efficiency in tracking the theoretical optimal power profiles for online battery control.

important_claims.claim: Online optimization of flow rate reduces parasitic loss and enhances instantaneous system efficiency.
important_claims.evidence: In the first stage, optimal flow rate is obtained based on online optimization to reduce parasitic loss and enhance instantaneous system efficiency.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a reusable multi-physics VRB modeling framework and a dual-time-scale control architecture applicable to other flow battery systems or grid-integrated energy storage with dynamic duty cycles.

keynotes.title: Vanadium Redox Flow Battery: Review and Perspective of 3D Electrodes

keynote.title: Vanadium Redox Flow Battery: Review and Perspective of 3D Electrodes
keynote.core_problem: Limited electrochemical activity of electrodes in vanadium redox reactions hinders high-performance VRFB operation.
contributions: Categorization of 3D electrodes derived from foam, biomass, and electrospun fibers
Discussion of flexible electrode design and compositional functionalization to enable high-speed mass transfer and abundant active sites
Review of 3D electrocatalysts—including metal-based, carbon-based, and composite materials—and their impact on surface charge distribution uniformity and stability
Synthesis of challenges and future perspectives for VRFB centered on 3D electrodes, 3D electrocatalysts, and reaction mechanisms

gap_signals.text: Limited electrochemical activity of the electrode in vanadium redox reactions
Need to assess advancements in electrodes to inspire innovative approaches for enhancing electrode structure and composition

important_claims.claim: Flexible electrode design and compositional functionalization create high-speed mass transfer channels and abundant active sites for vanadium redox reactions.
important_claims.evidence: Stated as a functional outcome of the design approach; no experimental evidence provided in abstract/excerpt.

important_claims.claim: Strong interaction and ordered arrangement of 3D nanocomposites influence uniformity and stability of surface charge distribution, enhancing electrochemical performance.
important_claims.evidence: Stated as a mechanistic assertion; no empirical validation detailed in abstract/excerpt.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a taxonomic framework for 3D electrode materials and electrocatalyst classes in VRFB; identifies structural and compositional levers (e.g., mass transfer channels, active site density, charge distribution control) for future electrode engineering—useful for materials selection, review synthesis, and gap-driven design in flow battery research.

comparison.prompt_assign_task: implemented as persistent create_task DAG entries with assigned agent, phase, dependency, deliverable, priority, and acceptance criteria
comparison.prompt_review_output: implemented as reviewer_gate plus boxue_final dependency gates; Boxue can add revision tasks via the task system
comparison.prompt_synthesize: implemented as bianlun_synthesis, paperwriter_draft, and boxue_final synthesis tasks
comparison.prompt_adjust_plan: implemented operationally by creating additional tasks or new delegation plans after reviewing outputs
comparison.prompt_finalize: implemented as boxue_final decision task, not as specialist execution

stronger_than_old_pipeline_tasks: role-specific tasks instead of generic phase tasks
risk constraints embedded in every task
optional teammate spawning for unblocked specialist work
prompt_alignment.remaining_manual_gate: Boxue still needs to review outputs and decide whether to create revision tasks; this preserves human/lead control over shared project state.

coordination_policy.boxue_role: decompose, assign, review, synthesize, adjust, finalize
coordination_policy.specialist_role: execute only the assigned scientific subtask
coordination_policy.shared_state: state-changing PaperGraph/project updates should be gated by lead/synthesis tasks

tasks.title: Build grounded PaperGraph evidence substrate
acceptance_criteria: At least one verifiable source or an explicit retrieval-failure report
Method/scenario/benchmark/contribution/limitation fields are populated or flagged unknown
Unsupported claims are marked for human review

tasks.title: Rank high-value knowledge gaps
acceptance_criteria: Every reported gap has supporting references or is flagged as ungrounded
No more than 10 ranked gaps
Each gap includes novelty/value/feasibility rationale

tasks.title: Generate and evolve gap-grounded hypotheses
acceptance_criteria: Each hypothesis links to a gap_id
Each hypothesis contains mechanism and falsification condition
Overlap/novelty risk is reported

tasks.title: Run Socratic critique
acceptance_criteria: At least one counterexample per hypothesis
At least one alternative explanation per hypothesis
Actionable revision or rejection recommendation

tasks.title: Moderate structured debate and synthesize refined hypothesis
acceptance_criteria: Arguments are separated into factual vs conceptual disagreements
Refined hypothesis lists improvements from critique
Remaining risks are explicit

tasks.title: Audit mechanism fidelity
acceptance_criteria: All three verification layers are addressed
Regime-shift conditions are explicit
High-risk mechanisms are routed to revision or human review

tasks.title: Design falsifiable validation protocol
acceptance_criteria: At least one standard and one strong baseline
Metrics have success thresholds
Falsification criteria are stated before execution

tasks.title: Implement reproducible experiment
acceptance_criteria: Code runs or failure is diagnosed with logs
Random seeds and dependencies are documented
Outputs are saved in reproducible artifacts

tasks.title: Analyze experiment outcomes
acceptance_criteria: Distinguishes supported/refuted/inconclusive
Reports effect size or practical significance
Failed experiments are documented as negative knowledge

tasks.title: Draft evidence-grounded research plan/manuscript
acceptance_criteria: Claims are backed by results or citations
Limitations and failed paths are included
References are traceable to PaperGraph or retrieval artifacts

tasks.title: Run automated peer review gate
acceptance_criteria: Scores include specific justifications
Citation and claim/result alignment are checked
Revision actions are concrete

tasks.title: Synthesize final decision and next round
acceptance_criteria: Decision references specialist outputs
Knowledge-gap lifecycle status is updated
Next actions are either finalize or explicit revision tasks

boxue_delegation_plans.next_step: Let unblocked specialist tasks run first. Boxue should review outputs at dependency gates, then adjust or unlock downstream phases.

references: D. Kolomiiets et al. (2022) SPECIFIC FEATURES OF HYDROMECHANICAL SYNTHESIS OF VANADIUM PENTAOXIDE NANOBELTS doi:10.31651/2076-5851-2022-3-14

evidence_type_annotations.claim: hydromechanical synthesis with intensive magnetic stirring

Demonstration that mechanical forces under ambient aqueous conditions—mediated by NaCl and turbulent hydrodynamics—can drive anisotropic nanobelt formation and crystallinity control in V₂O₅ without thermal post-processing.
Transition to predominantly amorphous or nanocrystalline phase after 96-hour synthesis, indicating loss of long-range crystalline order.
Full text excerpt:
The research aims to develop a scalable, ambient-condition methodology to produce nanostructured materials for advanced energy storage applications.

The experimental procedure involved monitoring the evolution of physicochemical properties over time, enabling detailed insight into the crystallization and growth behavior under turbulent liquid-phase conditions.
The observed changes in electrical conductivity confirmed the dissolution of V₂O₅ and the subsequent saturation of the solution.

references: Yanbo Zhang et al. (2025) Interfacial Engineering of MXene‐Based Heterogeneous Membranes with Enhanced Ion Selectivity in Vanadium Redox Flow Battery doi:10.1002/adfm.202510597

evidence_type_annotations.claim: heterostructure catalyst

Rational interfacial engineering enables enhanced ion selectivity and proton conductivity in MXene-based heterogeneous membranes for VRFBs, via heterointerface proton transport channels and pH-responsive Donnan exclusion.
However, previous studies on membranes primarily focus on enhancing the selective‐conductive properties of the secondary phase while neglecting interfacial regulation.
Full text excerpt:

In this study, PFSA/CF3SO3‐M heterogeneous membrane is successfully fabricated by incorporating CF3SO3‐M nanosheets—derived from organic Lewis acid (trifluoromethanesulfonic acid) etching of Ti3AlC2—into perfluorosulfonic acid resin (PFSA).

Electrochemical evaluations demonstrate that the PFSA/CF3SO3‐M membrane outperforms conventional Nafion membrane in vanadium redox flow battery (VRFB) applications, achieving 98.1% coulombic efficiency and 84.2% energy efficiency after 800 cycles.

references: Zhenyu Wang et al. (2026) Balanced-state electrolytes overcome crossover in vanadium redox flow batteries doi:10.1038/s41467-026-70872-8

evidence_type_annotations.claim: balanced-state electrolytes achieves a 75
Balanced-state electrolytes overcome active species crossover, enabling precise control over transmembrane ion flux and maintaining dynamic equilibrium of active species.

Abstract: Flow batteries are a leading large-scale energy storage technology, valued for inherent safety and scalability. However, active species cross over the membrane—beyond intended charge carriers— resulting in rapid capacity decay and hindering further development. Traditional approaches to mi...[truncated]
Full text excerpt:
However, active species cross over the membrane—beyond intended charge carriers— resulting in rapid capacity decay and hindering further development.
Traditional approaches to mitigate capacity decay focus on increasing membrane ion selectivity, but this typically compromises power density.
This approach enables precise control over transmembrane ion flux, thereby maintaining the dynamic equilibrium of active species and effectively reducing capacity decay.

Vanadium flow battery tests demonstrate that this approach overcomes the traditional trade-off between proton conductivity and ion selectivity: a battery employing a 15 μm-thick Nafion membrane with balanced-state electrolytes achieves a 75.4% reduction in capacity decay rate— from 0.061% to 0.015%...[truncated]
This method shows the potential to lower the capital cost of a 1 MW/4 MWh flow battery system by over 41.7%.
Crucially, the balanced-state electrolyte approach circumvents existing membrane-related constraints in redox flow battery development and establishes a framework for advanced electrolyte design.

Here, authors propose a balanced state electrolyte strategy that overcomes crossovers, thereby circumventing existing membrane-related constraints and establishing a new framework for advanced electrolyte design.

references: Aumber Abbas et al. (2021) Effect of electrode porosity on the charge transfer in vanadium redox flow battery doi:10.1016/J.JPOWSOUR.2020.229411

evidence_type_annotations.claim: cyclic voltammetry | impedance spectroscopy | galvanostatic charge/discharge studies
Tailored electrode porosity substantially influences charge migration and significantly improves VRB performance.

Effect of electrode porosity on the charge transfer in vanadium redox flow battery Abstract Vanadium redox flow battery (VRB) has attracted enormous attention as a large-scale energy storage system owing to its long life, stable capacity and high safety.
Full text excerpt:
This work reports the effect of electrode porosity on the electrocatalytic activity and charge transfer in VRB using spent tea derived porous carbon as electrode material.

The modified electrode with high degree of porosity improves the charge transfer process and exhibits lower charge transfer resistance with higher electrocatalytic activity for vanadium redox reactions.
The results suggest that the tailored porosity substantially influences the charge migration and significantly improves the VRB performance.
Full text excerpt: Tailored electrode porosity substantially influences charge migration and significantly improves VRB performance.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: the redox targeting concept to tackle the above issues

Surface-immobilized redox catalysts with matched redox potentials enable selective, efficient interfacial charge transfer for vanadium species, improving energy efficiency and stability across current densities.

Redox-targeted catalysis for vanadium redox-flow batteries Abstract Vanadium redox-flow battery (VRB) as a promising electrochemical power source for large-scale energy storage, suffers from various polarization losses despite that it has been extensively studied in the past decades.
Full text excerpt:
In this study, we report an unprecedented method based on the redox targeting concept to tackle the above issues.

Upon operation, the reversible proton-coupled redox targeting reactions between PB and VO2+/VO2+ on cathode, PBA and V2+/V3+ on anode facilitate the interfacial charge transfer of the vanadium species and concomitantly inhibit the hydrogen and oxygen evolutions, which improves the selectivity of the...[truncated]

We anticipate the surface immobilized redox catalysis approach demonstrated here would generically provide a paradigm for improving the sluggish kinetic processes in a variety of electrochemical devices.

references: Zebo Huang et al. (2024) Experimental Validation of Side Reaction on Capacity Fade of Vanadium Redox Flow Battery doi:10.1149/1945-7111/ad1ec8

evidence_type_annotations.claim: charge cut-off voltage control
Demonstrates that optimizing charge cut-off voltage mitigates capacity fade caused by side reactions in VRFBs.

Experimental Validation of Side Reaction on Capacity Fade of Vanadium Redox Flow Battery The all-vanadium redox flow battery (VRFB) is widely regarded as the most effective solution for mitigating the intermittent nature of renewable energy sources and simultaneously achieving “carbon neutrality goa...[truncated]
Full text excerpt:

The all-vanadium redox flow battery (VRFB) is widely regarded as the most effective solution for mitigating the intermittent nature of renewable energy sources and simultaneously achieving “carbon neutrality goals.” Nevertheless, the battery’s overall performance is adversely affected by capacity lo...[truncated]
This study proposes a novel approach that aims to minimize side reactions and mitigate capacity fade by employing an appropriate charge cut-off voltage mechanism.

The experiment centers around the examination of performance indicators, including battery charge/discharge capabilities, internal resistance, capacity retention rate, and efficiency, for analytical purposes.
The findings indicate that by setting the charge cut-off reference voltage at 1.65 V, the battery’s capacity can be sustained at 61.76% after 60 cycles.

development of a coupled electro-thermal equivalent circuit model for VRB systems that accounts for electrochemical and thermal dynamics

evidence_type_annotations.claim: nanofluidic electrolytes
Nanofluidic electrolytes with 0.1 wt% MWCNT enhance energy storage capacity by 22% compared to pristine electrolyte in vanadium redox flow batteries.
Electrolyte containing 0.2 wt% MWCNT induces unwanted precipitation at the electrodes during electrochemical reaction.

The limitation of energy storage capacity in vanadium redox flow battery impedes further commercialization of the battery.
We conclude that nanofluidic electrolytes can considerably improve the energy storage capacity with optimized content of MWCNT.
